title: Mahjong at the Derby: How to Host a Mint Julep + Mahjong Party
description: A practical host's guide to throwing a Kentucky Derby–themed mahjong party. Menu, drinks, hat rules, playlist, and printable bet cards — everything you need to pull it off.
author: MahjNearMe
body:
The first Saturday in May is one of those rare calendar moments that rewards theatrics. Big hats, big bourbon, big personalities. It's also, conveniently, the perfect excuse to gather four friends around a mahjong table and call it an occasion.

Derby + mahjong is a combination that shouldn't work but absolutely does. Both reward patience, pattern recognition, and a willingness to make increasingly questionable decisions as the afternoon wears on. If you've been looking for a reason to host a mahjong party that doesn't feel like a weekly game, this is it.

Here's how to do it right.

Timing: aim for a 2 PM start

The Kentucky Derby post time is typically around 6:45 PM ET. That gives you an unusually long runway for a themed party. Start at 2 PM with tiles on the table, break at 5:30 for the undercard races and mint juleps, and play the Derby itself as a live break between hands.

If your group is competitive, this structure works beautifully — the race becomes a natural intermission, and everyone's back at the table by 7:15 with strong opinions about which horse cost them money.

Dress code: hats required, kitten heels optional

The single cheapest way to make a party feel like an event is a dress code, and the Derby gives you one for free. "Hats required" is the rule. Anything else is negotiable.

Tell guests in the invite: floppy, feathered, fascinator, or just a nice straw — it doesn't have to be expensive. Dollar-store flowers plus a hot-glue gun plus a thrift store sunhat will carry the mood. Some hosts make the hat itself the cover charge.

The bar: juleps, naturally

The Mint Julep is the official Derby cocktail, and it's genuinely easy to batch. For eight drinks:

  • 2 cups Kentucky bourbon (Buffalo Trace and Four Roses are both good under-\$40 picks)
  • 1 cup fresh mint leaves, lightly muddled
  • ½ cup simple syrup (equal parts sugar and water, dissolved)
  • Crushed ice
  • Mint sprig garnish

Stir, strain, serve over crushed ice in silver cups or rocks glasses. Make one batch before guests arrive, one more right before the race.

Non-drinkers: a "virgin julep" of ginger ale, lemon, mint, and a dash of bitters holds up.

The food: heavy apps, no full meal

You don't want a sit-down dinner cutting into game time. Plan for grazing:

  • Pimiento cheese with crackers — Southern, easy, cheap
  • Deviled eggs — timeless, and easy to pick up one-handed while playing
  • Benedictine dip (Kentucky's cucumber-cream-cheese classic) with vegetables
  • Hot brown bites (turkey + bacon + Mornay on toast points) — the derby-appropriate flex
  • Derby pie or bourbon pecan bars for dessert

Put everything on the sideboard, not the mahjong table. You don't want Benedictine dip on the bams.

The playlist

Low-key Southern. Nothing that requires dancing. Dolly Parton, Bonnie Raitt, Bill Withers, a little Loretta Lynn. When you get closer to post time, put on the NBC broadcast — the pre-race pomp is part of the draw.

Betting: keep it light

You're not running a casino. A small pool works best. Options that don't require anyone to know what an exacta is:

  • Draw-a-horse. Write every horse's name on a slip, put them in a hat, everyone draws one. Winner takes the pot.
  • Fantasy Derby. Each guest picks a horse based on name alone. Winner takes a small bottle of bourbon or a new mahjong tile pouch.
  • Favorite color jockey silks. Seriously. It works.

The mahjong part

Keep the game structure loose. Two hands before the undercard races, a pause for the Derby, three hands after. Whatever style your group plays (American, Chinese, or Riichi), this isn't the day for a tournament — it's a day for the version of the game that makes room for a conversation about somebody's hat.

If you're mixing experience levels, this is actually a great party for a beginner to join. Themed chaos makes the learning curve feel less steep.

If you don't have a group yet

It's the first Saturday in May, not December — you still have time to put one together. A few ways:

  • Post in your neighborhood Facebook group looking for three players. Derby + mahjong is a unique enough pitch to draw interest.
  • Check if your local mahjong group is doing a themed event. Many do.
  • If you're in Louisville, Lexington, or anywhere in the broader Ohio Valley, Derby-themed mahjong events are easier to find than you'd think. Our Kentucky listings are the obvious starting point.

A final note on hats

Everyone always underestimates how much taller their hat will be once they sit down and lean over the table. Warn your guests, or plan for at least one hat-versus-chandelier moment. It's half the fun.

Gate's open at post time. Happy Derby, and may your juleps be cold and your hands be concealed.

Looking for a mahjong group to join for the Derby — or after? Find one near you on MahjNearMe. Listings are free and updated weekly.
